coredata.dc:title: Coconut husk-lignin derived carbon dots incorporated carrageenan based functional film for intelligent food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Sangeetha, U.K.
Sudhakaran, Nandhana
Parvathy, P.A.
Abraham, Malini
Das, Subrata
De, Sriparna
Sahoo, Sushanta K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) derived from sustainable natural feed-stocks like lignin have gained wide acceptance by virtue of their renewability and promising potential in intelligent sensing applications. The precursor lignin is isolated from agro-biomass waste, coconut husk through sodium hydroxide based extraction process. CDs are synthesised from amine functionalized lignin through solvothermal process and integrated into carrageenan biopolymer matrix (1, 2 and 3 wt%). The composite film with 2 wt% CDs (CARR2CD) showed optimum fluorescent emission intensity, excellent pH dependent fluorescent color change in the food pH range, reasonable tensile strength (46.50 ± 1.32 MPa) and 27 % increase in elongation at break. CDs imparted UV-light blocking properties (70 % UV-light) and enhanced hydrophobicity of the carrageenan matrix. CARR2CD film showed 84 % visible light transparency, 79 % reduction in oxygen transmittance rate (OTR), 81 % reduction in CO2 gas permeability and excellent antioxidant and antibacterial properties (against E. coli and S. aureus). As a practical application, the developed responsive packaging material is used to track pH change associated with milk spoilage via noticeable color change in fluorescent emission of the composite film. Thus, the developed responsive composite film paves a way for use as green and sustainable transparent intelligent food packaging material.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) derived from sustainable natural feed-stocks like lignin have gained wide acceptance by virtue of their renewability and promising potential in intelligent sensing applications. The precursor lignin is isolated from agro-biomass waste, coconut husk through sodium hydroxide based extraction process. CDs are synthesised from amine functionalized lignin through solvothermal process and integrated into carrageenan biopolymer matrix (1, 2 and 3 wt%). The composite film with 2 wt% CDs (CARR2CD) showed optimum fluorescent emission intensity, excellent pH dependent fluorescent color change in the food pH range, reasonable tensile strength (46.50 ± 1.32 MPa) and 27 % increase in elongation at break. CDs imparted UV-light blocking properties (70 % UV-light) and enhanced hydrophobicity of the carrageenan matrix. CARR2CD film showed 84 % visible light transparency, 79 % reduction in oxygen transmittance rate (OTR), 81 % reduction in CO2 gas permeability and excellent antioxidant and antibacterial properties (against E. coli and S. aureus). As a practical application, the developed responsive packaging material is used to track pH change associated with milk spoilage via noticeable color change in fluorescent emission of the composite film. Thus, the developed responsive composite film paves a way for use as green and sustainable transparent intelligent food packaging material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable packaging provides long-term viability of environmental resources by preventing food products' deterioration, reducing wastage, and protecting foods until usage. Intelligent packaging, an innovative concept emerged in the field of packaging lets the monitoring of condition of the packaged food or its surrounding [1]. pH-responsive materials, a key class of intelligent systems can find great potential in the field of food packaging, since the rotting of many food items is greatly associated with a change in pH of the material occurring due to bacterial action [2,3]. This responsiveness can be integrated into the packaging material through the incorporation of indicators, either colorimetric or fluorescent into a polymer matrix [4]. In this sense natural indicators are mostly preferred due to their important characteristics of non-toxicity, availability, compatibility, and environmental friendliness over their synthetic counterparts (e.g. bromophenol blue, chlorophenol red, bromocresol purple, etc.), which are carcinogenic in nature. Natural sourced colorimetric indicators such as anthocyanin, alizarin, shikonin, etc. are able to sense pH effectively. However, these bio-sourced color indicators are not stable as their responsiveness is greatly affected by factors such as temperature, atmosphere, and UV light [5,6].

In this regard, the detection of any kind of stimuli on the basis of fluorescence provides more accuracy and higher sensitivity in responsiveness through significant change in emission. Inorganic and organic fluorescent materials are not preferred for food packaging due to migration issues and high toxicity [7]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of bio-sourced material in the carbon family with inherent high quantum yield, fluorescent emission property, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent photostability [8]. With abundant functional groups on their surface to interact with the surroundings, and owing to their unique properties like antioxidant, anti-ultraviolet, antibacterial, biocompatibility, biodegradability, tunable emission properties and sensitivity, CDs have a great potential in smart packaging [9–11], [12]. CDs addition is also reported to improve the mechanical, UV-blocking, thermal stability, and barrier properties of packaging films along with imparting active properties to the material [13]. Lin et al. developed a chitosan-based packaging film containing nitrogen-doped CDs derived from citric acid and urea, in which a significant change in emission intensity is noticed with increase in pH of pork from 5.77 to 6.84 with time [14]. Similarly, Roy et al. [13], Cheng et al. [2] and Khan et al. [15] also prepared CDs incorporated biopolymer films for either active or intelligent food packaging.

Considering the sustainability of materials, CD's synthesis from residual biomass appeared as an intriguing strategy for green smart packaging [16]. Coconut husk, an abundantly generated agricultural byproduct from the coconut industry (20 million tons a year globally), is a lignocellulosic material that contain higher amount of lignin biopolymer (30–45 %) compared to all other biomass [17–19]. Lignin extraction from coconut husk and its effective utilisation will help to address its disposal issue and open a way for waste to wealth conversion. Lignin is a largely occurring aromatic biopolymer consisting of p-coumaryl, coniferyl and sinapyl alcohols as the monomeric units [20,21] and exhibit antibacterial, antioxidant and UV absorption properties. Soda pulping process is an efficient and cost effective extraction method for producing lignin compared to other existing processes (kraft, lignosulfonate, organosolve, etc.) owing to its sulfur free nature and high purity [22]. Lignin based carbon quantum dot-PVA composite films developed by Tao et al. exhibited excellent fluorescence performance and pH responsiveness for intelligent sensing such as sweat monitoring [23]. Lin et al. fabricated a probe for the rapid detection of biogenic amines with a low detection limit of 0.045 μM by combining poplar saw dust lignin CDs and pyromellitic acid CDs, and incorporating them into a cellulose nanofiber matrix [24].

During the last decade, polymeric materials from renewable resources have gained huge attention over petroleum-based polymers for making packaging films due to its compostability, biocompatibility, sustainability, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness in the industrial point of view [13,25–27]. κ-carrageenan is an anionic sulfated polysaccharide isolated from widely available rhodophyta red seaweed species, and has abundant hydroxyl groups capable of forming strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding during film formation [28]. Properties like relatively low cost, food compatibility, antibacterial properties, and home compostability further make carrageenan an efficient system for real world food packaging applications [29]. Incorporation of antioxidant, UV blocking and antibacterial properties into the polymer system without sacrificing mechanical and barrier properties can lead to the development of efficient packaging material using biodegradable polymers.

In the present work, pH responsive CDs with potential for intelligent packaging application is synthesised for the first time sourced from a widely generated agro biomass, coconut husk. Alkali lignin is extracted from coconut husk and subsequently functionalised to form CDs through solvothermal process. Meta phenylene diamine is used for amination of lignin through mannich reaction before converting to fluorescent CDs for successful amine grafting in the lignin backbone to provide efficient pH responsiveness. Apart from pH sensing, developed nanomaterial also offer antibacterial and antioxidant properties to the system for food packaging. The structure, fluorescent properties, pH responsiveness, morphology and functional groups of CDs are investigated in detail and incorporated in carrageenan biopolymer system. Carrageenan-CD composite film showed excellent pH responsive fluorescent property along with significant reduction in oxygen permeability and noticeable improvement in antioxidant, UV blocking and antibacterial properties, which has not been explored so far. Further, the prepared film is used to monitor the food freshness to see the application feasibility in commercial beverage packaging.

Coconut husk for the extraction of lignin was collected from a local market situated in Thiruvananthapuram, India. Sodium hydroxide pellets (low chloride content), formaldehyde (37 %), sulphuric acid (98 %), methanol and N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) were obtained from Merck's EMPARTA. K- carrageenan, alkali lignin (TCI lignin), ascorbic acid and ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) were brought from TCI Chemicals India Pvt. Ltd. and hydrochloric acid was purchased from Rankem Chemicals. M-phenylene diamine (MPDA), 2,2-diphenyl -1-picryl hydrazyl (DPPH), fluorescein diacetate (FDA), propidium iodide (PI), quinine sulfate and glycerol (99.5 %) were procured from Sigma Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. All chemicals were used as received. E. coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895 and S. aureus ATCC 25923 were procured from NCCL, Pune for antimicrobial assay. Cow milk for freshness monitoring was brought from a local market in Thiruvananthapuram, India.

As the initial step, coconut husk was grinded using a natural fibre grinding machine (Amey Engineers) and screened through mesh size of 1 mm. Sodium hydroxide based extraction process was used to obtain lignin from coconut husk powder by adopting the method published by Chaudhary et al. [17] (Fig. 1 ). Herein, 12 g of husk powder was treated with 240 mL 2 wt% NaOH solution and stirred at 140 °C and 200 rpm for 5 h. After cooling, the reacted mixture was filtered and thoroughly washed with distilled water. Solid part containing cellulosic residue was separated to yield dark brown coloured solution containing extracted lignin. Collected solution was then acidified to an approximate pH value of 1 using sulphuric acid, boiled for 1 h and kept overnight. The obtained precipitate was then collected using centrifugation process and washed in order to neutralise the medium and to remove the salt formed in the precipitation process. Lignin thus obtained was dried in an air oven for 24 h at 50 °C (yield: 15 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalisation of lignin and carbon dot synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Coconut husk derived alkali lignin was aminated through mannich reaction (Fig. 1) by following a process published by Shi et al. with a slight modification [30]. 2 g coconut husk alkali lignin (CLIG) was dissolved in 10 mL of NaOH (1 M) solution made using deionised water and heated at 50 °C for 10 min. 1.1 g of meta phenylene diamine (MPDA) dissolved in 2 mL water was added to lignin solution and stirred for 5 min. Subsequently, 0.6 mL formaldehyde was added to the system and heated at 90 °C, 300 rpm for 4 h and then the yielded product was precipitated using HCl (1 M) after cooling down the solution. Precipitated product was centrifuged and washed well with water to remove any unreacted amine as well as chloride ions present in the system. Finally, pH was adjusted to ∼5–6 and the product was dried in an air oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

In the next step, 0.2 g of aminated lignin was mixed with 70 mL N,N-dimethyl formamide and stirred for 1 h at 80 °C. This solution was then transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal autoclave and kept in an air oven preheated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution part was filtered using 0.22 μm pore sized membrane filter and DMF was removed using rotary evaporator. CDs were re-dispersed in water and lyophilised to get a fine powder. (yield: 40 %).

Initially, 1 g of κ-carrageenan was added to 50 mL of water and stirred for half an hour at 55 °C for complete dissolution. After that 0.3 g of glycerol was added to the system and stirred again to acquire a homogeneous solution. CDs dispersions (0, 1, 2 and 3 wt% of carrageenan) were made in 3 mL of DI water through sonication and added to the initially prepared carrageenan solution. The mixture was stirred continuously until a homogeneous system was formed, and later poured in a petri dish and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 10 h to avoid shrinkage. Carrageenan film is named as CARR and composite films are designated as CARRXCD hereafter, where X denotes the CD's percentages in films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots (CDs) and its precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical composition of coconut husk was found out by following TAPPI-T-222 method and cellulose and hemi cellulose contents were determined following Yokoma et al. [31]. FTIR spectra were obtained using an ATR-FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) to confirm the structure and functionalisation of samples. 1H NMR spectra of lignin and aminated lignin as well as 31P NMR of coconut husk lignin (CLIG) and alkali lignin from TCI chemicals (TCI lignin) were recorded in 500 MHz Bruker Advance DPX spectrometer. The particle size of the synthesised CDs were analysed using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2, T30 S-TWIN) operated at an accelerating potential of 300 kV. TEM images were analysed using Gatan software and size distribution was found out using ImageJ software. X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out for different samples between diffraction angles (2θ) of 10° and 90° using Cu Kα radiation in Malvern Panalytical diffractometer. Debye Scherrer's Eq. (1) was used for calculating the average size of the particles.

Scherrer's formula is:
(1)
D =
K × λ
β × cos θ

where D is the average crystalline size, K is the Scherrer's constant (0.89), λ is the wavelength of X- Ray radiation used (0.154 nm), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of diffraction peak expressed in radians and θ is the Bragg angle [32].

Heteroatom incorporation of lignin through amination, and structural changes happened during carbonisation while preparing CDs were analysed using a PHI 5000 Versa probe Scanning Esca Microprobe (XPS) which uses a monochromatic Al Kα x-ray source with a focused beam (<10 μm to 300 μm). Obtained spectra was deconvoluted using Multipak XPS data processing software (PHI Inc.) and the binding energy was calibrated with respect to C1s peak at 284.8 eV.

The Raman spectra from samples were recorded using a custom-built multimodal spectroscopy system utilizing a 532 nm laser (Quantel Q-smart 450) for excitation in combination with a Czerny-Turner spectrograph (Andor Kymer 328i)-CCD detector (Andor iDus 420 A-BU2). The spectrograph has a 500 nm blazed grating with a groove density of 600 gr/mm, offering a resolution of 0.2 nm and a bandpass of 131 nm. The Raman spectra were recorded for a laser energy of 2 mJ and exposure time of 30 s, and the data were pre-processed through baseline removal and vector normalization. The Raman bands in the spectra were peak resolved by fitting the Gaussian function to accurately evaluate the data. The corresponding intensity ratios were calculated for the characterization of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties study and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis absorption spectra for CD solutions were recorded using UV–Vis spectrophotometer, UV-2401PC, Shimadzu, Japan. Fluorescence properties of the synthesised CDs and the fabricated films were investigated using a Yvon Fluorolog 3 spectrofluorimeter with a 450 W Xenon flash lamp as the exciting source. Fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesised CDs were measured using the same instrument by using quinine sulfate as the standard reference. The relative quantum yield was calculated using the Eq. (2) given below.
(2)
Φ s
=
Φ r
×
I S
I r
×
A r
A s
×
ɳ s
ɳ r
2
where Φ, I, A and ɳ are the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), integrated emission intensity, absorbance and refractive index of dispersion (1.33 for DI water) respectively. The subscripts “r” and “s” represent reference substance, quinine sulfate (with known QY value of 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) and sample respectively. Absorbance value at 350 nm was controlled below 0.05 to reduce the interference of reabsorption effects [33]. Fluorescence life times of the CDs in different pH medium were measured using a picosecond single photon counting system (Horiba, DeltaFlex). Surface charge of CDs in various pH conditions were tested using a zeta potential analyser (Anton Par, Litesizer). The pH conditions in the range of 1 to 11 were prepared using HCl and NaOH solutions in all studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and morphology analysis of films
ce:section.ce:para:
The color parameters of the composite films, ‘L’ value (lightness), ‘a’ value (red-green) and ‘b’ value (yellow-blue) were determined using Hunterlab colorimeter with a white plate as standard background. Total color difference (∆E), whiteness index (WI) and yellowness index (YI) were calculated using the Eqs. (3)-(5) respectively [13]. ∆L, ∆a and ∆b are the difference between each color value of composite films with the neat carrageenan film. Measurements were taken for three different samples of same composition and their average values were taken.
(3)
∆ E =
∆ L
2

∆ a
2

∆ b
2
(4)
WI = 100 −
100 − L
+
a 2
+
b 2
(5)
YI = 142.86 ∗ b / L
Measured color parameters were statistically analysed using one-way ANOVA in origin software. Tukey test was used for means comparison.

Samples were cut into 3 cm × 8 cm size and light transmittance through the film samples were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2401PC, Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 200-800 nm. UV barrier property and visible light transparency were quantified measuring percentage transmittance at 280 nm and 660 nm respectively [34]. Surface and cross-sectional morphology of the film samples were analysed using Carl Zeiss EVO MA18 FE SEM.

Moisture content present in the composite films were measured through oven drying method using Eq. (6). 2 cm × 2 cm sized samples (W0) were dried in a hot air oven at 105 °C until a constant film weight (W1) was reached. Moisture content was expressed as percentage of initial film weight [34].
(6)
Moisture content =
W 0

%

This dried sample (W1) was dipped in 25 mL water at 25 °C for 2 h as well as for 4 h and water solubility was calculated from the weight difference noticed after drying the recovered film (W2) using Eq. (7) [34].
(7)
Water Solubility

%
Water and milk contact angle measurements were carried out in Sessile drop method using a Contact angle giniometer (DSA 30E, Kruss Germany). All analysis was carried in triplicates.
Thickness of film samples were measured by a digital screw gauge with an accuracy of 0.001 mm. Measurements were taken at three different points and their average value was used for calculations.

Thermal stability of carrageenan film and its composites with CDs were analysed using thermogravimetric analyser (TGA/DTG 6300) by heating the samples from 30 to 700 °C in nitrogen atmosphere at a scanning rate of 10 °C/min [25].

Mechanical properties of films were tested using a universal testing machine following ASTM standard D882–88. Film was cut into the shape of rectangles with 90 mm length and 15 mm width (three samples for each composition). Cross head speed was set to 5 mm/min and gauge length was kept as 50 mm. Film strength in different humidity conditions were measured after equilibrating the sample in a chamber maintained at 60 % and 100 % RH conditions. A 500 g weight was hung on the film and its load bearing performance was analysed.

Oxygen Transmittance Rate (OTR) of the films were measured according to ISO:15105 standard in a Labthink Oxygen Transmission Rate Test system. Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR) of the films were measured by following ASTM E96 standard [35]. Mason jars were filled with dried desiccant and covered using a cap containing 5 cm diameter films. Overall weight gain of the samples were measured over time that occur due to the adsorption of water molecules on the desiccant that enter the jar through the membrane [36]. WVTR was calculated by Eq. (8).
(8)
WVTR =
k Permeation area
where k is the slope of water gain versus time. CO2 gas permeability was assessed using the variable-volume method. All gas permeation measurements were conducted under high pressure conditions at 20 bar (291 psi) and the experimental setup is shown in Supporting Information (SI) (Fig. S1).
All measurements were performed in triplicates.

Antioxidant potential of CDs and composite films were studied by DPPH (2,2′-diphenyl-1- picryl hydrazyl) free radical scavenging assay as well as ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) radical scavenging assay by following Roy et al. and Aalami et al. [13,37]. Ascorbic acid was chosen as a positive control for the antioxidant activity study.

Different concentration of CDs and film samples were added to 4 mL 100 μM methanolic solution of DPPH and absorbance was measured after incubation in a dark environment for 30 min. Radical scavenging activity was measured quantitatively by comparing the peak intensity of blank DPPH solution at 517 nm with that of antioxidant added sample. Antioxidant activity was calculated using Eq. (9).
(9)
% Scavenging activity =
A c
−
A s
A c
× 100
where Ac and As corresponds to the absorbance of DPPH free radicals at 517 nm in the absence and presence of analytes respectively.

ABTS radical solution was prepared by mixing 5 mL of 7 mM aqueous solution of ABTS with 88 μL of 140 mM potassium persulfate aqueous solution. ABTS decolorisation assay requires the production of ABTS chromophore through the oxidation of ABTS by potassium persulfate. The mixed solution was kept in dark for 16 h and diluted to an initial absorbance value of 0.7 ± 0.05 at 734 nm. The diluted solution was then mixed with different concentrations of CDs and different size of CARR and CARR2CD films. Antioxidant activity was calculated using Eq. (10).
(10)

A s

× 100
where Ac and As corresponds to the absorbance of ABTS free radicals at 734 nm in the absence and presence of analytes respectively.

The antibacterial activity of the synthesised composites was investigated against gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria. This study involved the collection of Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria during the early stationary phase, with the concentration set at 106 CFU/mL. In brief, bacterial cultures were grown in Luria Bertani (LB) agar medium at 37 °C, and the initial bacterial density was optimized at 600 nm wavelength. For the spread plate procedure, 15 μL of the bacterial solution was mixed with varying concentrations of the formulation, adjusted to a total volume of 150 μL, and incubated for 3 h. Cell counts on petri plates were then determined and expressed as percentages, with control plates serving as the reference (set at 100 %). The bacterial solution was transferred to LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h [38].

To scrutinize bacterial cell morphology, E. coli and S. aureus cells were exposed to the formulation for 24 h. The treated cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer, and a 25 μL aliquot of the washed cell suspension was diluted in an eppendorf tube containing 2 mL of water. Subsequently, using a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution, the diluted cells were placed onto coverslips and fixed for 2 h . The fixed cells underwent sequential dehydration using aqueous ethanol solutions of different concentrations (50 %, 70 %, 85 %, 90 %, and 100 %) for 10 min each. Finally, all samples were dried under vacuum conditions for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. To prepare SEM specimens, the coverslips with bacterial cells were gold sputter-coated and then placed in the chamber of the scanning electron microscope (Model: ZEISS EVO-MA 10) for imaging. Further, live-dead analysis was performed to determine the viability of cells of Gram-negative E. coli using fluorescence microscopy. Bacterial cells were grown in LB media and were treated with nanocomposite films. The cells were stained with FDA (10 μg/mL) and were incubated for 30 min in the dark. Counterstaining was further carried out with propidium iodide (PI) (10 μg/mL). For fluorescent microscopy, the stained cells were analysed using a fluorescence microscope at 40× magnification (EVOS FL Cell Imaging System).

pH of the cow milk at different stages of spoilage was measured using OAKTON pH 700 pH meter. Developed packaging film was made into contact with fresh milk and fluorescence was measured using the instrument mentioned in Section 2.6 [39]. The same process was repeated by keeping milk at room temperature for 1, 2, 3 and 4 days respectively (here, keeping the milk outside the refrigerator speeds up its deterioration; if it was kept inside, it would have taken longer for reaching a low pH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure analysis of lignin and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Lignin was derived from coconut husk through base catalyzed extraction process and characterized for confirmation. Alkaline media results in the cleavage of bond between lignin and carbohydrate as well as the β-O-4 linkages, which leads to the solubilization of lignin. However, there is a possibility of repolymerization during extraction process where the intermediate formed during the process reacts with lignin nucleophile formed insitu and results in the formation of new bonds. Alkali stable enol ether formation and a byproduct, formaldehyde induced repolymerization is also possible while extraction using a nucleophile like OH− [40,41].

The FT-IR spectra of coconut husk, as depicted in Fig. 2(a), shows peaks corresponding to lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose present in it (composition analysis is given in Table S1 of SI). Bands ascribed to OH stretching (3340 cm−1), CH stretching (2920 cm−1), CO stretching (of acetyl ester in cellulose and carboxyl aldehyde in lignin, 1738 cm−1), aromatic skeletal vibrations (1400–1600 cm−1), C-O-C pyranose ring vibrations of glucose and C-O-C stretching of lignin (1040 cm−1) are clearly noticed to confirm the same. Lignin isolation reduces the amount of hydroxyl groups in the system due to the removal of cellulose and hemicellulose, which results in the reduction of –OH group vibrational peak intensity at around 3340 cm−1. The peak at 1040 cm−1 also diminished drastically due to effective removal of cellulose as well as the lignin β-O-4 linkage degradation during extraction process [17]. Deformed vibrations of aromatic CH bonds in the range of 1100 cm−1 became intensified after cellulose and hemicellulose removal. Alkali lignin extracted from coconut husk shows vibrational peaks which are characteristic of lignin biopolymer available commercially (Fig. S2 in SI) and reported in literature. Symmetric stretching and asymmetric stretching of CH bonds in methyl, methylene as well as methoxy groups accounts for the peaks in the range of 2900 cm−1. Carbonyl stretching vibrations of carboxylic acid resulted in a band peaked at 1707 cm−1 and aromatic skeletal vibrations related bands are centered at 1598 cm−1, 1510 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 [42]. Peaks at 1321 cm−1 and 1121 cm−1 are associated with syringyl rings, and those at 1265 cm−1 and 1027 cm−1 are related to guaiacyl rings [43]. The band ascribed to CO stretching arises from the aromatic ether moiety as well as phenolic hydroxyl groups of lignin skeleton is present at 1217 cm−1 [44,45].

31P NMR spectra of extracted lignin (CLIG) shows that the sample contains aliphatic OH, syringyl OH, guaiacyl OH, p-hydroxy phenyl and carboxylic acid OH groups in the structure [46]. Spectra observed for extracted lignin is comparable to that of lignin available commercially (TCI lignin) clearly observable from Fig. S3 in SI.

Vibrational peaks in aminated lignin (Fig. 2(a)) are comparable to that of lignin precursor indicating that the basic lignin skeleton remained unaltered during mannich reaction. A new peak appeared at 3216 cm−1 corresponding to the NH stretching vibration in the aminated system. NH bending vibration of NH2 groups at 1640 cm−1 got broadened due to overlapping with the aromatic skeletal vibration band [47]. Peak around 1600 cm−1 is observed to become intensified owing to the incorporation of more aromatic rings in the system because of the aromatic diamine attachment.

The 1H NMR analysis is carried for further confirmation of alkali lignin formation and corresponding spectra is shown in Fig. 2(b(i)). Feeble peaks observed at 2 ppm in the spectra corresponds to the methyl protons present in the vicinity of double bonds or carbonyl groups in the lignin [48] and strong peak appearing at 2.51 ppm is attributed to the dmso-d6 solvent. Methoxy protons accounts for the peak at 3.4 ppm and aliphatic hydroxyl group proton appears at 3.75 ppm. β-O-4 linkage and β-5 linkage in the lignin structure are attributed to peaks at 4.87 ppm and 5.32 ppm respectively [42,48]. Spectra signals from 6 to 7.8 ppm are assigned to the aromatic rings present in the lignin which include syringyl, guaiacyl and p-hydroxyphenyl units [49]. Similar findings for the remaining peaks are reported in literature [50,51]. To confirm the grafting of amine on the surface of lignin, NMR spectra of functionalized lignin is shown in Fig. 2(b(ii)). A peak corresponding to newly introduced methylene bridge in the form of Ar-CH2-N appear as an overlapped peak with methoxyl and hydroxyl proton in between 3.4 and 3.7 ppm which confirms the amine functionalization [52]. Extra peaks appear in the aromatic region also, which is due to the introduction of new aromatic rings in the system.

The FT-IR spectra of CDs derived from the aminated lignin (Fig. 2(a)) involves almost all peaks in the precursor. Introduction of CN bonds during the graphitisation process must be the reason for the appearance of a shoulder peak at 1652 cm−1 which leads to the total broadening of the peak in that frequency range. One new peak observed at 1740 cm−1 is due to the formation of new carbonyl moieties during the hydrothermal reaction. Peak intensity at 1213 cm−1 as well as 1030 cm−1 got increased mostly due to the formation of more CO bonds owing to the condensation of hydroxyl groups.

Micrographs of aminated lignin CDs along with their size distributions are investigated through HR-TEM, and the results are depicted in Fig. 2(c). CDs exhibited a quasi-spherical morphology with size distribution in the range 1.5 nm to 5 nm in aqueous dispersion with average size of around 3.3 nm and possess crystalline structure with well resolved lattice fringes. Size is comparable to the lignin based CDs synthesised by Zhu et al. [33]. As reported in other biomass derived lignin based CDs, lattice spacing of 0.23 nm is observed which correspond to the (100) plane of the graphite like crystalline structure [23,53]. The crystalline structure of CDs as observed in HR-TEM analysis supports the XRD spectra shown in Fig. 2(d). Coconut husk XRD spectra contain major peaks at 15.96° and 22.02° (2θ). Former one corresponds to the amorphous phase of cellulose and hemicellulose whereas the latter one accounts for the crystalline phase of cellulose and lignin. It is noticed that XRD spectra of alkali lignin is partially broad with a peak centered around 2θ ∼ 21.12° owing to its amorphous nature which is very similar to what observed in TCI Lignin (Fig. S4 in SI). Peaks corresponding to cellulose and hemicellulose are seen to have completely disappeared after lignin extraction which indicates the successful removal of these components during lignin isolation. Amination with meta phenylene diamine broadened the spectra which is ascribed to increase in amorphous content in the material, that resulted in the increase of Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) as shown in Table 1 . Noticeably, in case of CDs the corresponding peak became narrow with a significant reduction in the FWHM value with d spacing of 0.4 nm ascribed to (002) plane in graphite (JCPDS Card No. 75–1621) [54]. Crystallite size increased upon carbonisation due to the graphite like structure formation. CDs are generally reported to display broad powder XRD peaks and high inter planar distance due to the amorphous nature induced by the surface functionalities and disordered arrangement of carbon atoms [24], the obtained peak position and shape are comparable to the lignin CDs reported by Tao et al. [23]. Thus, the XRD results further confirm the structure of CDs synthesised are similar to CDs derived previously from alkali lignin [55].

Fig. 3(a) and (b) shows XPS survey spectrum of coconut husk and alkali lignin (CLIG) respectively. It is noticeable that husk contains significant amount of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements while extracted lignin (CLIG) contain only negligible amount of nitrogen in it. The XPS survey spectrum of ACLIG (Fig. 3(c)) further confirms the amination of alkali lignin via tracing the presence of nitrogen (6.9 %) along with carbon (75.3 %) and oxygen (17.8 %) elements in the studied material. The high resolution C1s spectra of ACLIG, as shown in Fig. 3(e), has been precisely deconvoluted to the peaks related to C-C/C=C (284.5 eV), CN (285.89 eV), CO (287.06 eV) and CO (288.46 eV) functional groups. N1s spectra contain two major peaks at 399.01 eV and 400.36 eV corresponding to CN and NH bonds respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra confirms the existence of Ph-C=O (530.8 eV), C=O/ HO-C=O (532.58 eV) and C-O/C-O-C (533.09 eV) functionalities in the material [56]. XPS spectra of CD (Fig. 3(d)) is observed to be comparable with that of ACLIG. The nitrogen concentration in the CDs was found to be higher than that in the precursor, which might be because of the participation of the solvent dimethyl formamide in the carbonisation process. The C1s spectra show four peaks in CD (Fig. 3(f)) which are closely attributed to 284.45 eV (C-C/C=C), 285.67 eV (CN), 287.23 eV (CO) and 288.86 eV (C=N/C=O) [30,57]. Meanwhile, relative percentage of CN bond as well as CO bond increased during carbonisation which can be correlated with the IR spectra result showed earlier. O1s spectrum further confirm three kinds of bonds present in ACLIG (Ph-C=O at 531.38 eV, C=O/ HO-C=O at 532.77 eV) and C-O/C-O-C at 533.67 eV). Deconvoluted spectra of N1s in CD shows three peaks corresponding to pyridinic, amine and pyrrolic moieties at 398.38 eV, 399.75 eV and 400.42 eV respectively [58]. A quantitative analysis, as shown in Table 2 , reveals the that the synthesised CD possess extensively conjugated SP2 domains with nitrogen atoms in the core and different functional groups on the surface [33].

Raman spectra (Fig. S5 in SI) give further insights into the graphitisation happened during hydrothermal reaction. Raman peaks centered at 1385 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1 corresponds to D and G bands that are attributed to SP3 and SP2 carbons in the material. It is noticed that ratio of intensity of G band to that of D band increased upon CD formation from ACLIG which indicates that graphitisation happened and SP2 carbon concentration increased in the system after CDs formation [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysing the photophysical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical response of CDs is crucial to be analysed for their applications in the field of responsive packaging. As seen in the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 4(a)) a major peak appears at around 250–280 nm range corresponding to the π → π* transitions of aromatic CC present in the SP2 core of CDs. Peaks appearing in the range of around 300–400 nm are due to the n → π* transitions of functional groups present in the surface [33,60]. The absorption peaks centered at 298 nm and 368 nm can be ascribed to n → π* transition of CO and CN functionalities respectively which are identified in the system through functional group analysis [61]. CDs dispersed in water showed blue color under a 365 nm UV source.

Fluorescence emission properties of the synthesised CDs were investigated at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 290–450 nm (Fig. 4(b) and (c)). Until an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the emission maxima remained almost unchanged and appeared around 445 nm, however, the emission intensity was observed to increase with the increase in excitation wavelength. The emission spectral intensity reached a maximum under 350 nm excitation and after that it showed a decreasing trend at higher excitation along with red shift in emission peak positions. The excitation dependent emission is a common characteristic property of CDs and is attributed to the presence of surface defects occurring due to the presence of hetero atoms like oxygen and nitrogen [53,62,63]. The emission band is asymmetric in nature with an extended tail at the long wavelength owing to the diversified surface states present in the CD particles [61]. The excitation spectra recorded at the emission wavelength at 445 nm indicates that emission is mainly originated from the absorption region corresponding to surface state (Fig. 4(a)). CDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) value of 6 % upon excitation with a wavelength of 350 nm which is higher than the reported QY of lignin based CD by Tao et al. [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on CDs emission
ce:section.ce:para: pH responsive fluorescent property of the CDs is desirable to develop intelligent packaging. Thus, change in photoluminescence properties of CDs in different pH environments were examined and the resulted spectra are shown in Fig. 4(d). It is apparent from the fluorescence spectra that CD's emission band contain two different spectral regions; one centred at 490 nm (green) and the other at 440 nm (blue). In a highly acidic condition (pH ∼ 1), green band is observed to be prominent than blue which is also reflected in the color of solution that appears green under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4(g)). With the increase in pH value, the blue band intensity gradually increased and green band got diminished with an overall blue shift in overall emission peak, which is clearly noticeable in the normalised emission spectra, as shown in Fig. 4(e). However, no significant change is observed in the emission wavelength as pH is further enhanced from 5 to 7. There is a sharp increment trend in the overall emission intensity of the combined emission peak with the increase in pH value till it reached pH ∼ 5. Thereafter, some irregular trend is observed in the fluorescent intensity without any significant change in emission peak wavelength. Appearance of CD solution in different pH media under the day light as well as under 365 nm UV lamp are shown in Fig. 4(g) and the corresponding CIE diagram of pH dependent emissions are shown in Fig. 4(f). The results reveal that the fluorescence property of aminated lignin CDs is highly pH dependent, particularly in the acidic environment, which is the most probable range of pH change associated with food spoilage. Similar fact can be correlated with the photophysical properties exhibited by N-doped CDs synthesised from tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane and citric acid [64]. This can be explained based on the functionalities (CO, OH-C=O, CN, NH2 and OH) attached to CDs which have a conjugated structural core. The functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen are more inclined to the pH of the medium due to protonation and deprotonation processes. The inter and intra molecular hydrogen bonding present in the system are greatly influenced by the pH induced deprotonation and can cause vibrational coupling of these functional groups with sp2 domain, which can lead to energy level broadening [65]. Further, pH variations may additionally affect the electron availability of the nitrogen contained in the carbogenic core (e.g. pyridinic nitrogen), which leads to a shift in the band gap of the electronic transition as discussed in case of chitosan- ortho phenylenediamine system reported by cheng et al. [2,66]. Intensity increment with pH increase in the acidic range mentioned earlier can be attributed to the deprotonation associated protective shell of negative charge development which lower the non-radiative recombination rate. Whereas in the basic range, highly stable protective shell is usually formed that resulted in stable low non radiative recombination associated with constant emission intensity. Reduction in emission full width at half maximum (FWHM) with the increase in basicity of the medium might be the outcome of complex surface states on inner charge transfer and energy bands [64]. Comparison of the emission properties of CLIG based CD with that of commercially available lignin is shown in Fig. S6 in SI. Table S2 in SI compares CDs synthesised in our study to previously published CD systems with pH-dependent fluorescence change used for intelligent sensing applications.

The time resolved emission characteristics of CDs were also monitored in different pH conditions after exciting the sample solutions at 330 nm to achieve more insights about the influence of pH on the fluorescence emission mechanisms (Fig. 5(a)). Decay time data of CDs in all pH conditions fitted very well to a tri-exponential function (with chi square value ≈1) and the results are listed in Table 3 . The origin of individual decay components are debatable in case of CDs [65,67]. Diverse fluorophores or energy levels present in the CD material may result in multiple lifetimes in the same system [61]. Intensity weighted average lifetime of CDs' solution increased with increase in pH of the medium until pH 5 and then remained constant. All three modes of emissions are significantly active at highest H+ concentration. Fractional intensity of T1 and T2 decreased while that for T3 increased with increase in pH of the medium which indicates that the transition corresponding to T1 or T2 is the reason behind the green emission and that corresponding to T3 for blue emission. Such distinct and well-isolated lifetimes indicated that the obtained CDs have appropriate emission-lifetime values to be used for pH-sensing applications.

In order to get a clear reason behind pH-dependent PL emission, the relationship between zeta potential and pH of the system has been studied and plotted in Fig. 5(b). CDs in highly acidic medium (pH 1) showed positive zeta potential which decreased upon an increase in pH of the medium. In media having pH >2, zeta potential showed negative value progressively. All the functional groups such as amine, imine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl grafted to CDs got protonated in the highly acidic condition and deprotonated with a decrease in acidity. This change is pronounced particularly in the pH range from 1 to 5 which can be well correlated to the pH-responsive emission property of CDs. The isoelectric point of CDs lies in between pH 2 and 3 similar to amino acids which indicates that CDs are amphoteric in nature as reported earlier [59]. Thus, zeta potential measurement further confirms that the environment changes of CDs account for its sensitivity towards pH variation.

Color and transparency of films are important for packaging application since they can influence the appearance of packed food materials [14,68]. Photographs and transmittance spectra of carrageenan- CD composite films are presented in Fig. 6(a) and (b). Neat carrageenan film is colorless, which got slightly brownish after the incorporation of CDs into the system. Still, the composite films are quite transparent with easy readability underneath. The calculated color values of all films are listed in Table 4 which indicates that with increase in CDs content, a net color difference (∆E) occurred along with a reduction in whiteness index (WI) and raised values of yellowness index (YI) in composite films with a significant change in the color values (p < 0.05) [13,14]. In order to check the visible light transparency, the transmittance of the films was measured at 660 nm. Carrageenan film showed the highest transparency with 92.5 % transmittance while CDs addition reduced the values to 84.4 %, 78.8 %, and 73.5 % for CARR1CD, CARR2CD, and CARR3CD films, respectively.

One of the essential features of a packaging film is its ability to block UV rays, which can prevent high-fat foods from losing their quality due to photocatalytic oxidation. Transmittance of composite films at 280 nm has been analysed to evaluate their UV barrier properties. Carrageenan film is observed to be highly transparent to the harmful UV rays (71.2 % transmittance), while composite films with 1 %, 2 % and 3 % CDs showed highly reduced UV transmittance of 43.7 %, 30.1 % and 27.3 % respectively. The observed UV blocking property of composite films is attributed to the UV blocking property of a chromophore present in lignin based CDs [69]. This result signifies that CDs added films possess potent UV barrier property compared to the pristine carrageenan films, which is highly desirable for food packaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM analysis of the fabricated films
ce:section.ce:para: As carrageenan has good film-forming ability, SEM micrograph of neat carrageenan film shows smoothness on the surface as well as cross-sectional areas without any cracks (Fig. 7 ). The addition of 1 % CDs did not show any significant change in their morphology. On further increase in the concentration of CDs, the surface roughness is found to increase along with a change in cross-sectional surface morphology in CARR2CD and CARR3CD films [13,70]. Wrinkles are noticed on the surface of CARR3CD films revealing the significant increase in the surface roughness. However, no micro-cracks or phase separation is noticed on the surface or cross-section confirming good compatibility and a stronger CD-polymer interfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR analysis, water resistance and contact angle
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of carrageenan-CD composite films are given in Fig. S7 in SI and moisture content and water solubility test results are given in Table S3 in SI. Moisture content and water solubility of carrageenan is not altered much after CDs incorporation, a negligible reduction in interaction with water observed until 2 % CD concentration.

Water contact angle (WCA) measurement was mainly carried out to study the wettability and hydrophobicity of films and the results are shown in Fig. 6(c). Carrageenan is generally hydrophilic due to an abundance in hydroxyl groups and hence showed water contact angle of 42.7 ± 1.8°. The addition of ligninbased CDs into the system imparted hydrophobicity to the material and followed an increasing trend of WCA up to 72.1 ± 2.7° with the increment in CDs concentrations. This might be due to an increase in surface roughness and hydrophobicity imparted by CDs. Roughness contribution can also be well correlated with the morphology analysis.

Table 5 shows that the film thickness increased upon increase in CDs concentration. Fig. 6(d) illustrates the tensile stress-strain curves of carrageenan and corresponding composite films with CDs. The mechanical properties of a film are essentially determined by intermolecular and intramolecular interactions between distinct polymer chains present in the matrix. Carrageenan exhibited a tensile strength value of 55.25 ± 0.13 MPa and elongation at break (EAB) of 17.79 ± 1.08 %, wherein, CDs incorporation reduced the tensile strength and increased the elongation, at 3 % CD addition tensile strength reached 35.13 ± 0.18 MPa and elongation at break reached 27.13 ± 0.06 % as shown in Table 5. Young's modulus (YM) and elastic modulus (EM) values also decreased with increase in CDs concentration. This decreasing trend in tensile strength and modulus is due to the occupancy of nanosized CDs between the carrageenan polymer chains and reduction in intermolecular forces between polymer chains within matrix. CD might have created an easy sliding pathway for polymer chains due to its plasticizing effect on carrageenan polymer chains. Sazzadur Rahman and coworkers reported similar system where glucose-glycerol CDs reduced tensile strength and increased EAB of guar gum‑sodium alginate biopolymer system [9]. Comparable results were also observed in mushroom based CDs in gelatin carrageenan polymer blend system [13]. However, the strengths are comparable to the traditional packaging materials like LDPE, PP, PLA, etc. CARR2CD films were equilibrated in two different humidity conditions, 60 % RH and 100 % RH and its strength was analysed by hanging 500 g weight on it (Fig. S8 (a)). Mechanical properties of the films were observed to be maintained in high humidity environment as well.

TGA and DTG thermogram of carrageenan and its composite films are depicted in Fig. 6(e) and (f) in order to investigate their thermal stability. It is noticed that all films undergo decomposition in multiple stages corresponding to different chemical bonds in the system. The weight loss occurred within 100 °C is due to the evaporation of moisture which is present through hydrogen bonding with the hydroxyl group of carrageenan polymer chain and glycerol [71]. Glycerol degradation resulted in a weight loss starting from 150 to 200 °C in the system. The major degradation starts at 224 °C which corresponds to the elimination of –SO3 − groups attached to the polymeric backbone of carrageenan followed by carbohydrate backbone fragmentation until 400 °C. Thermal stability got slightly improved on incorporation of CDs into the polymer system. It is important to note that CDs addition reduce the moisture content as observed in the initial stage of TGA curve as well. As seen in Fig. 6(f) multiple peaks are noticed in DTG thermogram confirming the multiple steps of degradation. The peak corresponding to maximum rate of degradation (Tp max) almost remained unchanged after the addition of CDs. Onset of degradation temperature for carbohydrate backbone breakage increased as a function of CDs concentration mostly due to the formation of strong intermolecular interactions between the biopolymer and the incorporated CDs [13]. Table 6 shows that at 3 % CDs concentration, there is a reduction in thermal parameters due to agglomeration of CDs in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence analysis of the fabricated films
ce:section.ce:para: For real time packaging applications, fluorescence property of composite films was studied and the emission spectra is shown in Fig. 8(a). Maximum emission intensity is observed in carrageenan composite film with 2 % CD. Higher loading of CDs results in a reduction of emission intensity which might be due to the aggregation of CDs within the film [14]. Thus CARR2CD is selected for further analysis meant for responsive packaging.

An interesting fact to be noted is that the fluorescence emission behavior of the film is very much similar to CDs solution. Emission of CARR2CD film in the excitation wavelength range of 290 nm to 450 nm was measured and the corresponding emission spectra is shown in Fig. 8(b). Fig. 8(c) shows emission spectra of CARR2CD film (excitation wavelength 350 nm) monitored in different pH conditions and it showed a similar trend of change in response to the surrounding pH as observed in case of CDs dispersion. Only the green band peak position in the emission band of film is relatively blue shifted compared to CD dispersion in aqueous media which might have aroused due to the new type of hydrogen bonding interactions formed between polymer matrix and CDs as reported in PVA-CD system [23]. CIE diagram and photograph of composite film showing pH induced emission color change are shown in Fig. 8(d) and (e), respectively.

Moisture, oxygen and CO2 barrier properties of films are the primarily important parameters in food packaging applications which in turn depends on material composition, pore size distribution and interconnectivity of pores. High oxygen permeability can lead to lipid oxidation, discoloration, microbial activity enhancement, and aging in food materials [72]. Similarly, moisture leads to the growth of microorganisms and subsequent food spoilage with time. The WVTR and OTR values of neat carrageenan film and optimized CARR2CD film are shown in Fig. 9(a). The OTR value of carrageenan film is estimated to be reduced from 397.1 ± 2.1 cm3/(m2.day) to 83.3 ± 2.3 cm3/(m2.day) on CD addition (79 % reduction) to the polymer matrix. Incorporation of nano fillers introduce torturous pathway for gas molecules and inhibit the permeation of oxygen through the film, thereby enhancing the oxygen barrier properties of the materials [73]. Further the interfacial bonding between the CDs and polymer contribute to the improvement in oxygen barrier property. Previous studies also confirm the reduction in OTR value on incorporation of CDS to the biopolymer system. Ezati et al. found that urea-glucose based CDs addition to cellulose nanofiber film decreased OTR value of the neat film [74].

The WVTR of carrageenan film reduced slightly with the addition of CDs, CARR and CARR2CD systems exhibited WVTR values of 258.3 ± 1.3 and 248.6 ± 1.8 g/(m2.day), respectively. This can be correlated with increase in hydrophobicity imparted by CDs in the films as observed in WCA analysis. It confirms that CDs have good compatibility with the polymer matrix without leaving any nano or micro separation. A similar result has been reported in the case of mushroom derived CDs added into gelatin-carrageenan based polymer matrix wherein the WVTR remained almost constant after the addition of CDs [13].

The measured CO2 gas permeability values of CARR and CARR2CD films are given in Fig. 9(b). CO2 gas permeability of the CARR membrane is found to be 1.86 × 10−11 ± 2.39 × 10−12 GPU. Wherein, the CARR2CD membrane exhibited an order of magnitude lower CO2 gas permeability of 3.51 × 10−12 ± 8.59 × 10−13 GPU than CARR membrane. The findings indicate that a substantial decrease (81 %) in CO2 permeability is achieved by adding just 2 % of CDs to the carrageenan system. Alves et al. previously reported a 50 % decrease in CO2 gas permeability after incorporation of organically modified nanoclay (1 %, no change with increase in nano content further) into carrageenan-pectin blend film system [75]. The significant reduction in gas permeability achieved can be attributed to the effect of increased tortuosity due to CDs incorporation.

CDs are reported to have high antioxidant activity and are utilized in packaging films as an active component [13,76]. Free radicals can accelerate the oxidation of lipids in food materials and this can affect the shelf life of packaged materials. DPPH & ABTS free radical scavenging assays are used in this study to investigate the antioxidant property of CDs derived from lignin. Free radical scavenging activity of aminated lignin CDs as a function of its concentration in methanolic solution of DPPH and aqueous solution of ABTS are shown in Fig. 9(c). Scavenging activity changed in a dose-dependent manner against both radicals and the half-maximal inhibition effective concentration value (EC50) is calculated as 167 μg/ml against DPPH and 81 μg/ml against ABTS radicals. We compared our CDs with ascorbic acid in terms of their antioxidant activities. CDs were observed to show more activity against ABTS compared to DPPH radicals due to comparatively high solubility of CDs in water. Polyphenols present in lignin are reported to play a major role in reducing the free radicals existing in a system and reducing oxidative stress [69,77]. Further, free amino groups enhance the antioxidant activity of the material. Also, it is reported that doped electron donating sites and sp2 carbon core of CDs perform an essential role in the destruction of free radicals by the formation of adducts which causes spin delocalization across the conjugated backbone of CD [78]. The antioxidant activity of biopolymer films is important in the field of food packaging since it can increase the shelf life of packed food products [79]. Antioxidant study of the CARR and CARR2CD films were done using DPPH and ABTS radical solutions for four different sized film samples and the results are shown in Fig. 9(d) and (e). CDs addition significantly enhanced the antioxidant activity of carrageenan matrix. Interestingly, size increment resulted in an increased antioxidant activity of CD added films at a higher rate compared to pure carrageenan matrix. Difference in the extend of radical scavenging against ABTS and DPPH is due to the faster release of CDs in aqueous ABTS solution compared to methanolic solution of DPPH. Results show that CDs retain their antioxidant activity even after the composite formation and this activity increases in a size dependent manner in the film. Absorption spectra of measurements are given in S8 (b) and S8 (c) of SI and p value calculation for antioxidant ability of CDs against DPPH and ABTS are given in Table S4 of SI.

The correlation between the concentration of CDs and their antimicrobial activity is a critical aspect of their effectiveness in packaging applications [80]. The samples are scrutinized using zone of inhibition study against gram negative and gram-positive bacteria.

CDs showed excellent antibacterial activity against both E. coli and S. aureus as shown in Fig. 10(a). It is noticeable that CD-carrageenan composite films exhibited more pronounced antibacterial activity than pure carrageenan film. Fig. 10(a) elucidated that the antimicrobial activity of CDs is concentration dependent. As the concentration of CDs increases, their ability to inhibit the growth of microorganisms also increases. In case of both bacteria, control set did not reveal any antimicrobial potency resulting in the no zone of inhibition. Whereas in case of composites, significant zone of inhibition is observed which might be due to the efficiency of CDs to adsorb onto the cell surface and disrupt the cell membrane by inducing oxidative stress followed by DNA damage [81]. Lignin precursor has abundant phenolic hydroxyl and methoxy groups in its structure which are reported to impart antibacterial activity [82]. Additionally, amine functionalities present in lignin derived CDs further enhance the antibacterial property of the samples [83].

The antimicrobial efficacy can be further monitored by SEM micrographs wherein distinct morphological damage inflicted upon bacteria by carbon dot (CD) nanocomposites can be attributed to several key mechanisms resulting from the interaction between CDs and microbial cells. As depicted in Fig. 10(b) and (c), with increase in CD concentration distinct morphological alteration can be monitored wherein there is a total functional loss in bacterial cell membrane caused by adsorption of CD molecules onto the cell surface. This adsorption destabilizes the membrane structure, leading to disruptions in its integrity. After treatment with CARR3CD, no structural integrity was found, cells are aggregated subsequently and no such prominent architecture is visible. This is possibly due to the leakage of vital intracellular components, such as ions and macromolecules, which compromises on the bacterial cell's ability to maintain its structural integrity and homeostasis. Furthermore, the nanoscale nature of CDs allows them to penetrate the bacterial cells easily. Once reach inside, the CDs can interact with intracellular structures, causing further damage. The accumulation of CDs within the cytoplasm can lead to swelling and distortion of the bacterial cell, which can ultimately result in its rupture. CDs' ability to disrupt the cell membrane, induce oxidative stress, and penetrate the cells themselves collectively contribute to the profound morphological abnormalities observed in treated cells [84]. The observed results indicate that CD anchored carrageenan nanocomposites is more effective against Gram-negative than the Gram-positive bacteria. These findings might be due to the bacterial cell wall composition wherein gram positive bacteria contain thick layered peptidoglycan layer as compared to gram negative one (S. aureus).

The Live/dead assay is a valuable tool for studying antimicrobial efficacy in a variety of contexts. To further quantify the antimicrobial mechanism and viability assessment purpose this study was carried out. It is evident from the above findings (Fig. 10(d)) that after treatment with CARR3CD nanocomposites more dead cells are visualized, which decipher to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by CDs under light exposure. This is particularly relevant for CD nanocomposites, which can be activated by light, leading to increased ROS production. ROS, including singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals, exert oxidative stress on bacterial cells, damaging cellular components like proteins, lipids and DNA. These oxidative insults disrupt vital cellular functions, leading to morphological abnormalities and eventual cell death. Antimicrobial study indicates that CDs added carrageenan films have potent antibacterial efficiency which can prevent the spoilage of packaged food materials.

Finally, the CARR2CD film has been applied to monitor the freshness of milk in order to study its applicability in packaging industry. It is well known that milk is a balanced food that contains lactose, lipids, proteins, vitamins, mineral salts, and other nutrients. However, it is a great medium for the growth of microbes, which can easily ruin this food material. So milk is taken as the case study material to investigate the fluorescence responsiveness of packaging material. One of the key microorganisms involved in milk deterioration is acid-forming bacteria. It converts lactose to lactic acid and causes acidification of milk, lowering the pH from 6.7 to <4.5 [85]. Milk protein get coagulated due to lactic acid, transforming it into a semi-gel like substance at the end of spoilage. Freshness before consumption should be checked to avoid the intake of spoiled food material. Carrageenan-CD composite film is used to monitor freshness of packaged milk on the basis of the pH change associated with the spoilage process. Composite film was made into contact with fresh milk with pH 6.7 and its fluorescence emission was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Same process was followed for milk at different stage of spoiling where it was observed that emission got redshifted (Fig. 11(a)) with a significant change in the chromaticity values as depicted in the CIE diagram (Fig. 11(b)). Fig. 11(c) and (d) shows the real-time image of milk spoilage and cartoon regarding this packaging application respectively. Contact angle analysis carried out with milk (Fig. S9) showed that the composite films have a higher contact angle with milk compared to pure water owing to the presence of fat content in milk. The result indicate that carrageenan-CD composite film can be used for detecting liquid food deterioration associated with pH change in the acidic range as shown in the case of milk.

Nitrogen containing pH responsive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesised from aminated alkali lignin (derived from lignocellulosic coconut husk) through solvothermal process and characterized for its potential as a fluorescent indicator in intelligent packaging applications. In particular, FT-IR, HR-TEM, XRD, XPS and Raman analyses were carried out to confirm the structure, size and functional groups present in the CDs. Fluorescent emission study showed excellent pH responsiveness of CDs most prominently in the acidic range (pH of food<6)) with shift in emission maxima and variation in intensity. CDs incorporated carrageenan film (2 wt%) exhibited fluorescent color change in response to pH which reveals its potential for responsive packaging. CDs significantly increased film properties such as oxygen barrier (83.3 cm3/(m2.day)), UV blocking and elongation at break without much reduction in the tensile strength. Escalated antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli along with increase in antioxidant properties were observed for CD-carrageenan composite film which impart active characteristics to the packaging system. Real time applicability of the developed intelligent biopolymeric packaging material is tested in tracking milk spoilage associated with increase in acidity with time.
